stories.title: Cameron Young Leads Cadillac Championship After 8-Under 64 — Spieth Close Behind

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
Cameron Young went 8-under at Doral on Thursday. Bogey-free 64. One back is Jordan Spieth with a 65.

Meanwhile, Scottie Scheffler — the world No. 1 and betting favorite — finished at -1.

The Blue Monster bites back. 🐊 #CadillacChampionship #PGATour

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
Round 2 leaderboard heading into Friday at the Cadillac Championship:

1. Cameron Young (-8)
T2. Jordan Spieth (-7)
T2. Alex Smalley (-7)
4. Nick Taylor (-6)
5. Nico Echavarria (-5)

Watch it live on Golf Channel starting at 3 PM ET. 🏌️ #CadillacChampionship

facebook.long_form:
The Blue Monster is back — and it already drew first blood against golf's biggest name.

Cameron Young made it look easy at Trump National Doral on Thursday, posting a bogey-free 8-under 64 to lead Round 1 of the 2026 Cadillac Championship. Young made birdie after birdie and didn't slip once against a Blue Monster course that has historically punished everyone who comes through.

Jordan Spieth sits one back after firing a 65 that included several strong iron shots and a putting performance that got the crowd buzzing. Spieth and Alex Smalley share T2 at 7-under.

And then there's Scottie Scheffler. The world No. 1 started brilliantly — three birdies in his first five holes — and then the Blue Monster showed its teeth. Back-to-back bogeys on holes 10 and 11 derailed the round. Scheffler didn't make another birdie the rest of the way, finishing at -1. He's seven strokes back in a $20 million event with no cut.

Round 2 is today (Friday) on Golf Channel at 3PM ET. Scheffler needs a serious charge to get back in it.
facebook.image_caption: Cameron Young: 8-under, bogey-free 64. Scheffler: +1 after a brutal back-nine implosion. The Blue Monster is back — and it's as dangerous as ever. #CadillacChampionship #PGATour

article.headline: Cameron Young Posts Bogey-Free 64 to Lead Cadillac Championship — Spieth One Back, Scheffler at -1

stories.title: LIV's Funding Crisis — Saudi Arabia Ends Support After 2026 Season, New Board Formed

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
BREAKING: Saudi Arabia's PIF is ending LIV Golf funding at the end of this season.

LIV has a new board and is seeking new investors.

Players are calling the PGA Tour.

The golf war has a new chapter. #LIVGolf #PGATour

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
Jordan Spieth on LIV losing Saudi funding:

"Too many unknowns for me to have a good gauge on what would happen."

"Olive branches were given out — Brooks took them up."

Measured. Quotable. Honest. #LIVGolf #PGATour

facebook.long_form:
The ground shifted under LIV Golf this week in a way nobody expected to happen this quickly.

Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund — the sovereign wealth fund that bankrolled LIV's entire existence since its 2022 launch — confirmed it will end its funding of the league at the conclusion of the 2026 season. The PIF has reportedly invested more than $5 billion into LIV over four years. That money tap is now closing.

LIV responded by forming a new independent board and announcing it is seeking new investors. The CEO maintained that the 2026 season continues as planned. But the broader question — what comes next? — has no clear answer.

The potential for a LIV-PGA Tour rapprochement is now very much on the table. Multiple player representatives for LIV golfers have already reached out to the PGA Tour. But the terms are not easy: Brooks Koepka, who returned last season, had to accept no equity grants for five years, a $5 million charity donation, and no bonus money in his first year back. Jon Rahm, Bryson DeChambeau, and Cam Smith were offered similar terms in February and declined. That offer may no longer be on the table.

Jordan Spieth summed up the broader sentiment at Doral Thursday: "There are just too many unknowns for me to have a good gauge on what would happen." He added that he was "glad not to be in the decision-making room."

Golf's great schism is entering its most uncertain chapter yet.
facebook.image_caption: Saudi Arabia is ending LIV Golf's funding after 2026. Players are calling the PGA Tour. The sport is at a crossroads. Golf's biggest off-course story just got even bigger. #LIVGolf #PGATour

article.headline: Saudi Arabia Ends LIV Golf Funding After 2026 — Golf's Biggest Business Story Reaches a Turning Point

stories.title: Korda Chasing Back-to-Back Wins at Riviera Maya Open — 5 Points from HOF

x_posts.label: Post 3-1
x_posts.text:
Nelly Korda won her 3rd career major last week at the Chevron Championship.

Now she's at the LPGA Riviera Maya Open in Mexico — and she's just 5 Hall of Fame points away from automatic induction.

She sits at T4 (-4) through Round 1. 🌟 #LPGA #NellyKorda

x_posts.label: Post 3-2
x_posts.text:
LPGA Riviera Maya Open R1 leaderboard:

T1. Brianna Do (-6)
T1. Melanie Green (-6) — had a hole-in-one 👏
3. Carlota Ciganda (-5)
T4. Nelly Korda (-4) and others

Korda sits 2 back. She's won the last major. Don't count her out. #LPGA

facebook.long_form:
A week ago, Nelly Korda was celebrating her third career major title. Now she's in Mexico, hunting her next win — and perhaps the most significant prize in her sport.

The world No. 1 sits at 4-under after Round 1 of the LPGA Riviera Maya Open at Mayakoba, two strokes behind co-leaders Brianna Do and Melanie Green, who each carded opening 66s at El Camaleon Golf Course. Green's round included a stunning hole-in-one. Carlota Ciganda is third at 5-under.

For context on why Korda's presence here matters beyond just the leaderboard: she now sits at 22 of the 27 LPGA Hall of Fame points required for automatic induction. Her victory at the Chevron Championship — a dominant, 5-shot win at -18 at Memorial Park Golf Course — earned her two more HOF points and reclaimed her Rolex World No. 1 ranking.

Five points remain. Multiple paths exist to get there. Every event Korda enters is now a potential Hall of Fame milestone moment.

At T4 through 18 holes at Riviera Maya, she's two shots back. That's not a deficit for a player who just won a major by five. Watch this leaderboard closely.
facebook.image_caption: Nelly Korda: 3 career majors. World No. 1. 22 of 27 Hall of Fame points. She's 2 shots off the lead at the Riviera Maya Open and hunting history. 🏌️‍♀️ #LPGA #NellyKorda

article.headline: Korda Five Points from LPGA Hall of Fame — World No. 1 Lurks Two Back at Riviera Maya Open

stories.title: Scheffler's Doral Disaster — World No. 1 Slumps to 71 in Round 1

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
Scottie Scheffler made three birdies in his first five holes at Doral.

Then went birdie-less for the rest of the round.

Back-to-back bogeys on 10-11. Finished -1 (71). Seven strokes off the lead.

The world No. 1 needs a low one today to get back in it. #CadillacChampionship

facebook.long_form:
Even the best player in the world has bad days at the Blue Monster.

Scottie Scheffler opened the 2026 Cadillac Championship with what looked like a promising round. Three birdies in his first five holes had the world No. 1 in red numbers early, and everything pointed toward another Scheffler clinic on a signature event stage.

Then holes 10 and 11 happened. Back-to-back bogeys halted the momentum. And after that? The putter went cold, the opportunities dried up, and Scheffler closed the day at -1 (71) — seven strokes behind Cameron Young's pace-setting 64.

In a 72-player field with no cut, Scheffler technically has nothing to fear about not finishing the tournament. But seven shots is a massive deficit in a no-cut event, because every player who made the field is capable of going low. To win, Scheffler likely needs a 62 or 63 on Friday just to get within striking distance.

It's the kind of opening round that would quietly eliminate most players. For Scheffler, it's a massive challenge.
facebook.image_caption: Scottie Scheffler: world No. 1, pre-tournament favorite — and sitting at -1 after Round 1. Seven strokes off the lead at Doral. The Blue Monster claims another victim. #CadillacChampionship #PGATour

article.headline: Scheffler Sits Seven Back After Round 1 Stumble — World No. 1 Needs a Career Round to Catch Up

stories.title: Spieth's Doral Resurgence — 65 and LIV Comments Put Him in Spotlight

x_posts.label: Post 5-1
x_posts.text:
Jordan Spieth shot 65 at the Blue Monster on Thursday and is sitting one off the lead at the Cadillac Championship.

New ball. New equipment. And the old Spieth magic starting to look very familiar again. 🏌️

#CadillacChampionship #PGATour

x_posts.label: Post 5-2
x_posts.text:
Spieth on the LIV situation: "I'm glad I'm not in the decision-making room."

Sometimes the most honest answer is the best one.

He's also T2 at the Cadillac. Good week so far, Jordan. 👏

#PGATour #LIVGolf

facebook.long_form:
Jordan Spieth had something to say at Doral on Thursday — and most of it happened with a golf club, not a microphone.

The three-time major champion fired a 65 on the Blue Monster course, making the card look easy with a combination of precision iron play and putting that recalled his best-level golf. He's tied for second at 7-under, one back of leader Cameron Young, and very much in the frame heading into Round 2.

Off the course, the bigger conversation was LIV Golf. With Saudi Arabia pulling its funding from the league and players' representatives reportedly calling the PGA Tour about potential returns, reporters naturally found Spieth. His response was measured, honest, and, typically, quite quotable.

"Olive branches were given out a couple months ago — Brooks took them up on it — so I'm not sure what would change now," Spieth said, referencing Koepka's negotiated return to the PGA Tour last year. "There are just too many unknowns for me to have a good gauge on what would happen. I'm glad I'm not in the decision-making room."

That quote — "glad I'm not in the decision-making room" — might be the best encapsulation of how most PGA Tour players feel right now. The situation is complicated, the stakes are high, and nobody wants to be the one holding the pen when the terms are negotiated.

For now, Spieth is focused on what he can control: a golf ball, a Blue Monster, and a chance to win.
facebook.image_caption: Jordan Spieth: 65 on Thursday at Doral. T2, one off the lead. New equipment. And the most quotable take on the LIV situation all week. Good to see you, Jordan. 🏌️ #CadillacChampionship #PGATour

article.headline: Spieth Fires 65 at Doral and Delivers the Week's Best Quote on LIV Golf's Collapse
